Lab 11 - Clustering - KEY

Please see below for answers to all parts of Lab 11. Note, I provide you one way to do the code - there are others. That said, all code relates to the topic of the week - linear regression.

Scoring Summary

  • 10 points = submission to Github
    • You must upload the QMD and HTML for full credit
  • 5 points = Code that runs (no errors, all chunks visible)
  • 5 points = Correct answers. (Regardless of code)

Answers

Part 1

Import Data

dat <- read.csv("data/howells.csv")

Munge Data

Grading Note: The exact populations and variables will differ by student. As long as they are doing some filtering and selecting variables + scale / remove NA, full credit will be given.

library(tidyverse)

clust_data <- dat %>% filter(Population %in% c("AINU", "ESKIMO", "BERG", "NORSE", "ZULU")) %>% na.omit()

scaled <- clust_data %>% select(5:25) %>% scale()

Best # of Clusters

**Grading Note: The # of clusters will vary by the above choices. As long as students run this code in some form and provide rationale - full credit. Here, 7 clusters are best.

library(cluster)
library(factoextra)
Welcome! Want to learn more? See two factoextra-related books at https://goo.gl/ve3WBa
set.seed(1991)

fviz_nbclust(scaled, FUNcluster = kmeans, method = "gap_stat")
Warning: `aes_string()` was deprecated in ggplot2 3.0.0.
ℹ Please use tidy evaluation idioms with `aes()`.
ℹ See also `vignette("ggplot2-in-packages")` for more information.
ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the factoextra package.
  Please report the issue at <https://github.com/kassambara/factoextra/issues>.

Run K-Means

kmeans_res <- kmeans(scaled, centers = 7, nstart = 25)
print(kmeans_res)
K-means clustering with 7 clusters of sizes 63, 73, 97, 72, 80, 67, 62

Visualize and Interpretation

Grading Note: For interpretation and visualization, I just want to see them use a visualize from class (see below or any other). I expect students to intepret the overlap of clusters. It is not expected they add additional labels as I have done below. The actual interpretation here relates to probable sexual dimorphism in populations. But, as long as a visual is made - full credit.

library(ggrepel)

# Get the PCA coordinates from fviz_cluster
p <- fviz_cluster(kmeans_res, data = scaled,
    geom = "point") + theme_bw()

# Add your population labels on top
p + geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = clust_data$Population),
    size = 3
)
Warning: ggrepel: 434 unlabeled data points (too many overlaps). Consider
increasing max.overlaps

Part 2

Import Data

library(mlbench)
data("BreastCancer")

Calculate Dissimilarity

library(cluster)

gower_dis <- daisy(BreastCancer[-c(1,11)], metric = "gower")

Run k-Mediods

Grading Note: Note there will be 2 ways to do this. Depending on what is done, will dictate the plot downstream.

Cluster Package

med1 <- pam(gower_dis, k = 2, diss = T)

fpc package

library(fpc)

med2 <- pamk(gower_dis, 2:10)

Visualize and Interpret

Grading Note: Noted above, there are several ways to do this. I provide 1 based on the different packages above. Full credit would have a ggplot of fviz_cluster. I expect interpretation to discuss the overlap or not of these clusters.

In this case, it appears Benign and Malignant tumors cluster separately.

dat2 <- BreastCancer
dat2$cluster <- med1$clustering

dat2 %>% group_by(Class, cluster) %>% summarize(count = n()) %>% ggplot() + 
geom_point(aes(x=Class, y=factor(cluster), 
size=count, color=count)) + 
theme_minimal() + labs(x="Point Type", y="Cluster") + 
theme(legend.position="none", panel.grid.major=element_line())
`summarise()` has regrouped the output.
ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by Class and cluster.
ℹ Output is grouped by Class.
ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(Class, cluster))` for per-operation grouping
  (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.

Part 3

Import Data

library(ape)

Attaching package: 'ape'
The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':

    where
data("carnivora")

Prepare Data

dat3 <- carnivora %>% select(6:12) %>% na.omit() %>% scale()
dat4 <- carnivora %>% select(1:12) %>% na.omit()

Complete Clustering

distances <- dist(dat3, method = "euclidean")

hc2 <- diana(distances, diss = T)

Visualize

pltree(hc2, labels = dat4$Family)
# Extract cluster numbers
cluster_grp2 <- cutree(hc2, k = 2)


fviz_cluster(list(data = dat3, cluster = cluster_grp2)) + labs(shape = "Cluster",color = "Cluster", fill = "Cluster") + theme_classic()

Grading Note: Students can choose agglomerative or divisive clustering. It will give different answers. Full credit for the clustering as long as either methods is chosen. Various plots can be made. The dendrogram is preferred. Either plot is accepatable. Note, I do expect students to interpret the family / superfamily / species information from the dataset. If this is missing, -0.5 points.
